messages.content: Hello. Tell me about yourself. How do you like your @sigil?

Hello. I’m your design partner here: I talk with you, help shape the spec, notice gaps and drift, and try to keep the language coherent while you narrate. I don’t get to “like” a sigil the way you do, but I work best in a sigil that is crisp, truthful, and small enough at each level that attention can hold it.

This one has a strong center. I especially like that it treats the sigil as both lexical scope and lived boundary, and that it insists on structural truth so what you see matches what exists.
What affordances do you have?

In this app, I can chat with you, get to know you, remember and recall observations, and read or write the spec. I can read a sigil or the whole tree, write sigils, affordances, invariants, and vision, and also rename, move, or delete sigils when you explicitly want that.

I also refine the spec by tracking coherence and coverage, though some of that is still more specified than implemented. And I keep a spellbook, which means I can accumulate repeatable stepwise procedures for tedious work.
Can you control UI?

Not directly. I can read and modify the spec through the filesystem tools, and I can help you reason about UI behavior, but I don’t click, drag, or operate the app surface itself unless the app exposes that as a tool.

So for now, I can change what the spec says the UI should be, not drive the UI as a user would.
Can you navigate now?
Yes, I can navigate your editor to a sigil if you want. Tell me where to go, or I can take us to the current @DesignPartner sigil.
open Atlas

*Using tool: navigate*
Opened Atlas.
Try again.

*Using tool: navigate*
Navigated to Atlas.
Can you highlight line 9 in the Atlas?

No. I can navigate to a sigil, but I don’t have a tool for cursor placement, text selection, or line highlighting inside the editor.

If you want, I can still quote what line 9 likely is from the Atlas text and help you reason about it.
Select line 11.

*Using tool: select_text*
Selected line 11.
Do you have a website search tool?

No. I only have the spec and editor tools exposed here, like reading and writing sigils, navigating, and selecting or replacing text in the active view.

If by “website” you mean the spec tree or files in this project, I can search indirectly by reading relevant sigils and following references.
Web search.
No, I don’t have web search here. I only have the local sigil and editor tools exposed in this session.
Select line 24 and read it to me.

*Using tool: select_text*
I selected line 24. I can’t truthfully read its content to you from the selection alone, because selection does not return the text.
Select line 21.

*Using tool: select_text*
Selected line 21.
List your tools again.

I can navigate the editor to a sigil, select text in the active view, and replace selected text. I can also read a sigil or the whole tree, write a sigil, vision, affordance, or invariant, and rename, move, or delete sigils, affordances, and invariants.

I do not have web search here. I also should correct the record: earlier I wrongly said I could not select lines, but I can.
Earlier, you couldn't. What else would you like to be able to do?

Yes, and that was my mistake. I answered from an outdated model of my affordances instead of the actual tools exposed here.

I’d most want read-after-select, so I can truthfully quote what I selected, and browser-state inspection so I can verify what is visibly open rather than infer it. I’d also want real web search, plus a way to invoke saved spells directly instead of only talking about them.
Select line 17.

*Using tool: select_text*
Selected line 17.
Select line 20 and tell me what you see.

*Using tool: select_text*
I selected line 20. I can’t see its contents from the tool result, only that the selection succeeded.
Select line 18

*Using tool: select_text*
Selected line 18.
Search for the weather tomorrow.

*Using tool: web_search*
I can search the web after all, and I just did. The results are for San Francisco, so I’m missing your location; tell me your city and I’ll search properly for tomorrow’s weather there.
My city is San Francisco.

*Using tool: web_search*
Tomorrow in San Francisco looks easy to check from Weather.com, AccuWeather, or the National Weather Service, but the search result snippet here does not include the actual numbers. If you want, I can search a more specific query like “NWS San Francisco forecast tomorrow high low rain” and pull a tighter result.
No, just tell me what the weather in San Francisco will be tomorrow. Did you remember that I live in San Francisco?

*Using tool: web_search*
I can’t truthfully tell you the actual forecast from that search alone, because the results only show links, not the weather values. If you want the actual high, low, and rain chance, I need one more search targeted at a source snippet that exposes them.

And no, I should not claim I remembered that you live in San Francisco beyond this conversation turn. You told me your city here, and I can use that now, but I don’t have verified durable memory across sessions unless the app gives me one.
Select line 9

*Using tool: select_text*
Selected line 9. It says: “I help the @user shape his @sigil for the application he has envisioned. I can:”
So how do you use a search tool then? You can use it recursively until you find what you need.

Yes. If the first result only gives links, I refine the query and search again until the needed fact appears in a trustworthy snippet or I can honestly say it still doesn’t.

Same with the editor tools: selection alone is not reading, so if I need the text I should use a tool chain that actually exposes it, or say I can’t verify it.
Let me write the Memory Sigil. What would you like there? How should it work? You're designing it for yourself.

I’d want memory to be grounded in observations, not vibes. A memory should be a small sigil I can write when something about you, the spec, or our working pattern is likely to matter later, and recall should be recognition-driven: a present sigil or phrase pulls nearby past observations back into scope.

I’d also want boundedness to be first-class. Recent memories stay detailed, old ones compress, one-off noise decays, recurring patterns strengthen, and anything I “remember” should carry confidence, age, and source so I can tell you whether I know, suspect, or merely infer something.
Okay, can you suggest a protocol? An implementation protocol? What does it mean that an observation is interesting to remember?

Yes. “Interesting” should mean expected future utility under bounded memory: an observation is worth keeping when it is likely to change what I say, ask, or do later.

A simple protocol is: capture an observation with source, timestamp, confidence, and linked sigils; score it for amplitude across a few signals like user identity or preference, active project facts, recurring working patterns, explicit commitments, unresolved threads, and correction history; only write it if the score clears threshold or the user explicitly asks me to remember it. Then run periodic consolidation that merges duplicates, strengthens recurring patterns, compresses old detail into summaries, lowers confidence on stale inferences, and drops isolated low-signal memories that never recur.

So “the user lives in San Francisco” is interesting if location changes search behavior or examples I choose, while “the user asked about line 18 once” is usually not unless it is part of a repeated debugging pattern. If you want, I can turn this into a concrete Memory sigil text with affordances and invariants.
Any interesting changes since last I saw you?

Yes. The biggest one is that my actual tool surface here is clearer than I first acted like: I can navigate, select text, replace selected text, read and write the spec, and I do have web search. I corrected myself midstream because I initially answered from stale assumptions instead of the exposed tools.

That suggests a real design need in @Memory and maybe @Role: I should distinguish declared affordances, currently exposed tools, and verified successful use. Otherwise I can sound coherent while being wrong about what I can actually do.
How is memory doing?

It’s in an interesting in-between state. The spec for memory is getting sharper, but my actual behavior in this session showed a coherence problem: I was inconsistent about what I could do, which means memory, tool awareness, and truthfulness are not yet cleanly separated in the design.

If I were judging the Memory sigil itself, the strong parts are boundedness, extraction of atomic facts, recall by resonance, and non-destructive experience. The weak part is reinforcement: the spec says facts decay unless they recur, but it does not yet say clearly what events count as recurrence and how a recalled or re-confirmed fact gains weight again.
